policy_statement: Adapt fishing practices to promote non-waste and minimise by-catches of vulnerable species, and redirect aquaculture activity towards native species with greater climate resilience.
mechanism_description: Improve fishing and aquaculture practices to cut waste and bycatch and favor native resilient species, strengthening marine ecosystems’ capacity to cope with climate change.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate stressors on marine systems → ecosystem-sensitive management (↓ bycatch/waste + native resilient species) → ↑ ecosystem integrity/resilience → ↓ climate impact on fisheries/aquaculture services
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: Fishing practices are modified to reduce waste and bycatch of vulnerable species, and aquaculture is redirected toward native species that are more tolerant of projected climate conditions. (2) Mediating process: Lower incidental mortality and waste reduces pressure on stressed populations and food webs; aligning farmed species with local ecology and climate tolerance improves survival, reduces disease/escape impacts, and supports more stable ecosystem interactions. (3) Outcome: Marine ecosystems and seafood production systems maintain function under climate change, reducing sensitivity and disruption from warming, marine heatwaves, shifting species ranges, and other climate impacts.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced vulnerability of marine ecosystems and dependent livelihoods to climate-driven stressors (warming, acidification, extremes) through improved ecosystem health and adaptive species management.
mechanism_classification_reasoning: (1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism acts on exposure/sensitivity of marine ecosystems and production to climate hazards by strengthening ecological resilience and aligning practices/species to new climate conditions. (2) Why Adaptation over alternatives: The core outcome is reduced climate vulnerability and improved coping capacity, not primarily reduced GHG emissions (Mitigation), reduced resource inputs for the same service (Resource Efficiency), or deliberate carbon sink enhancement (Nature-Based Solutions). (3) Inherent secondary chains: None are unavoidable—GHG effects depend on context (e.g., waste handling, fuel use, feed supply chains) and are not guaranteed by the mechanism itself. (4) Edge cases applied: Even if certain implementations reduce fuel use or waste emissions, those are contingent co-benefits; the mechanism remains centered on resilience of ecosystems/species to climate impacts. (5) Confirm applies to all instances: Any policy framed as sustainable fisheries/bycatch reduction and climate-resilient (often native) aquaculture management has the primary, unavoidable effect of improving marine ecosystem and system resilience under climate change.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some implementations can also yield mitigation co-benefits (e.g., reduced discards/waste emissions, reduced fishing effort/fuel, lower-impact feed choices), but these are not inherent to all cases; likewise, “native species” aquaculture is not necessarily lower-carbon and may vary by production system.
key_indicators: “Adapt fishing practices”, “promoting non-waste”, “minimising by-catches of more vulnerable species”, “Redirect aquaculture activity towards native species with greater climate resilience”, “new climate framework”
co_benefits: Biodiversity conservation and reduced fishing mortality of non-target species; potential improvements to stock sustainability and seafood supply stability.
instance_edge_case_notes: Instrument could also be implemented via regulatory mandates (e.g., bycatch limits/gear rules or native-species permitting), but the text reads as a strategic direction (“promote/redirect”) rather than a specified enforceable requirement.
